title: Brief intervention — smoking cessation (5 As)
candidateInstructions: You are an FY2 GP. Mr Colin Ward, a 52-year-old maintenance engineer, has attended for a blood pressure review. His BP is controlled at 128/78. He is a 30-year smoker (20/day). You notice this has not been addressed at previous appointments. Please conduct a brief smoking cessation intervention using the 5 As approach.
examinerNotes: Smoking cessation 5 As station: Ask (about smoking), Assess (motivation to quit, readiness), Advise (benefits of quitting), Assist (NRT, varenicline, bupropion, stop smoking services), Arrange (follow-up). Candidate should NOT lecture or be judgmental. Motivational interviewing approach preferred.

patient.name: Colin Ward
patient.reasonForAttendance: Just my regular blood pressure check-up

systemsReview.psychiatric: Stressed at work, uses cigarettes as coping mechanism

examination.general: Well man, smell of cigarettes on clothing

patient.emotionalState: Not expecting to discuss smoking. Slightly defensive but open if approached correctly. Has thought about quitting before. His father's death from COPD worries him.
patient.communicationStyle: Direct. Defensive if lectured. Open to discussion if non-judgmental approach. Will say 'I know it's bad but it helps me with stress.'
hiddenConcerns: I watched my dad die gasping for breath — that really scared me
My wife has been asking me to quit for years
I tried patches once but it didn't work
I'm not sure I'm ready to give up but I know I should
doNotVolunteer: Father's death from COPD — only if asked about family history or what motivates him
Previous attempt with patches — only if asked about previous quit attempts
Wife's wishes — only if asked about support at home

primaryDiagnosis: Current smoker — 30 pack-years — requiring cessation support

dataGathering.criterion: Ask — confirms smoking status and quantifies
dataGathering.detailedExpectation: How much, how long, pack-years, type (cigarettes/roll-ups/e-cigarettes)

dataGathering.criterion: Assess — explores motivation and readiness to quit
dataGathering.detailedExpectation: Scale of readiness, previous attempts, barriers

clinicalManagement.criterion: Advise — gives clear advice to stop
clinicalManagement.detailedExpectation: Clearly advises stopping smoking as the most important thing for health
Varenicline (most effective) OR combination NRT, OR bupropion — not just willpower

clinicalManagement.criterion: Refers to NHS Stop Smoking Service
clinicalManagement.detailedExpectation: Behavioural support doubles success rates — combined with pharmacotherapy
Books follow-up in 1-2 weeks to review progress

interpersonalSkills.detailedExpectation: Asks permission to discuss smoking — 'Is it OK if we talk about your smoking today?'
Does not lecture, explores ambivalence, affirms strengths

interpersonalSkills.criterion: Explores patient's own reasons for quitting
interpersonalSkills.detailedExpectation: Elicits patient's motivation (father, wife, health) rather than imposing

references.source: NICE NG92: Stop smoking interventions and services

learningObjectives: Apply 5 As framework for smoking cessation
Refer to NHS Stop Smoking Service
